data.title: This Is A Stock Picker's Market
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Next-gen investing experts Julia Ostian, Jack Bowman, and Kenio Fontes share their investment strategies (0:30) Rare earths and precious metals (2:20) Amazon, Apple, AMD and tech - concerns and excitement (9:00) Portfolio allocations (41:00)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome back to Investing Experts Podcast. Very, very excited to welcome back to the show Julia Ostian, Kenio Fontes, and Jack Bowman, three outstanding analysts we are fortunate enough to have on Seeking Alpha. They've been on the podcast before with Rob Isbitts. I'm very, very happy to have them back talking markets, talking stocks, talking whatever they want pretty much. Everybody, welcome to the show. It's great to have you.

Julia, we'll start with you. Do you want to introduce yourself and just remind listeners what you write about, where you write, things like that, your investment strategy.

Julia Ostian: Definitely. Hey, everyone. So I'm Julia. I started writing for Seeking Alpha pretty much a year ago, almost. And I'm mostly writing covering the tech sector and AI, of course, I guess everyone is interested in AI right now. And I'm not an exception.

I cover different sectors as well. But I would say probably the tech sector fascinates me the most. And I get the most excited about learning how things work, how companies progress, how they find new innovative ways to make money and that's really interesting and find new innovative ways to make money. And that's really interesting.

My investing strategy actually is focused heavily on momentum, on sentiment of the market.

I really believe that especially right now in this cycle, momentum is really, really important and ahead of Nvidia (NVDA) earnings, for example, right now, I really think that the company can show 60% revenue growth and the stock can tank just because people will be nervous or just because people wanted to see more or wanted more from this company.

So I am a true believer in the sentiment moving with the market. I trade sometimes here and there. I don't always hold my positions for a long time.

For example, right now I really think that the company can show 60% revenue growth and the stock can tank just because people will be nervous or just because people wanted to see more or wanted more from this company. So I am a true believer in this sentiment and moving with the market. trade sometimes here and there. I don't always hold.

Rena Sherbill: Much appreciated. Kenio, would you share with us how you invest, what you're looking at?

Kenio Fontes: Of course, thanks for having me here again. Kenio Fontes, Brazil based investor and I invest in a bit of everything with a focus on tech and retail but I'm always keeping on any stock that I find interesting and that can offer a compelling story.

I'm mostly looking for long-term investing, like 10 maybe 20 years from here. Of course I do some trades when stocks get too overvalued and mainly buy based on sentiment like Julia said but with a different focus.

I buy when the market is very pessimistic about something like Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) a few years ago or Amazon (AMZN) right now. I think that's pretty much my investing style

Rena Sherbill: Jack Bowman?

Jack Bowman: Thanks, so I'm probably known as the wonkiest person on our panel. I write a lot about macro and I do a lot of my investing strategy around macroeconomics. And so I'm making portfolio adjustments based on what the Fed is talking about, based on where GDP is going, and that's most of my trading.

Though I do some stock picking here and there. I like to dabble in material stocks like critical minerals and Rare Earths have been one of my focus lately. And then of course, I have some coverage of tech stocks because everyone's gotta love their tech stocks.

And I'm sure that we will talk about Amazon in just a moment because that has been on all of our minds, I'm sure.

Rena Sherbill: Jack, I was quoting you for a bunch of episodes about your take on Amazon and how Prime is not their moneymaker and AWS and building brand and what that means. And so, yeah, I've loved your guys' takes on Amazon.

Jack, I can get, why are you focused on Rare Earths or what led you to your choice of focus there brand and what that means and so yeah I've loved your guys's takes on Amazon. if I can get why are you focused on rare earths or what is what led you to your choice?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, so there has been maybe the last 30 years, this kind of sense in the macro environment of like the end of history. The Cold War ended, the Soviet Union was gone, and everything could be solved with globalization. Couldn't pay a domestic worker, pay a foreign worker. Couldn't buy domestic products, buy foreign products. And we did this to the extreme in rare earths.

And we woke up maybe two years ago realizing that China controls 90 % of rare earth processing. So we just can't make, like the amount of graphite and the amount of cobalt and things, we just can't process it here, but we still need it.

So now we're facing a world where in a trade war with China, they're weaponizing that against us. You don't want to play ball with us, you want to raise tariffs? Well, no rare earths for you. And that suddenly threw a giant wrench in our supply chains. And it's meant huge opportunity for domestic rare earth companies and Canadian rare earth companies in particular have been doing very well as we kind of re-industrialize that part of the sector that we let just kind of languish for a long, long time.

Julia Ostian: I have to say I really like this industry as well and I actually rely on Jack's articles for this myself. A year ago I have been in Indonesia and I like first hand I saw how China invests just loads of money in different countries to lock in those rare earths like everything basically anything they can get their hands on in different countries to lock in those rare earths like every two.

Basically anything they can get their hands on they will be locking in and I think it's a very very important topic and I think the US will really have a shortage in a couple of years when China feels more comfortable with like telling the US that they will be locking in and I think it's a very, very important topic and I think the US will really have a shortage in a couple of years when China feels more comfortable with telling the US that, listen guys, so now we have something that we also want from you, not only you want from us.

So yeah, I think it's a fascinating part of the market as well.

Rena Sherbill: It also sounds like shades of the AI with China and America competing and piggybacking and being exclusionary and yeah, interesting. Any companies that you would highlight or lowlight in the rare earths just as we're getting into things?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, the ultimate winner of all of this push so far with government meddling, so the US government has done a lot of meddling so far in the market. But they're buying up stockpiles now, they're trying to pass bills so they can just preemptively buy things.

They passed a floor where, so MP Materials (MP) is one of my big picks in this sector. They secured a contract with the Pentagon. The Pentagon buys these magnet components at almost double the going price on the broad market.

So MPs digging up stuff at $30 a ton. They're able to sell it for $50 a ton to the open market or to the government for $100 a ton. And this has created this kind of unfair part of the market where they're able to out-compete everybody on their margins because no one has a buyer like MP does.

(LAC), Lithium Americas is the other big one. They also have a government contract now. And they're one of the few Western companies producing lithium, which is a huge deal in battery production, as we may know.

Kenio Fontes: Can I ask a question for Jack? Do you have an opinion on gold? I wrote down an article about gold miners, I think it's a month ago, and it was very debatable, because my take was, I'm an individual investor, I'm not a head fund manager, so I don't think I have to beat the S&P (SP500) in the next quarter just in the next decade or five years so i'm not willing to bet that Barrick (B) and all these gold miners will be selling gold for $5k in the next year or two years, three years so I can be patient.

So what's your take on gold and all this momentum style versus long-term thinking?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, gold has been exceptional. It's been great for my portfolio, because I was a gold bug before all this, but now I'm looking at it saying, hey, we've run up so much. I saw a photo of a strip mall in China that had silver prices at $136, because they have a premium there. And it was like the most exuberant I had ever seen a precious metals market.

And so at this point, I'm not adding to my precious metals anymore. I'm sitting and waiting, which is a weird thing for me to say, because I have been banging the table for years about how much everyone needs gold, and all the investors in America are like, eschewing gold and refusing.

I think among miners right now, there's still some opportunity because they've lagged gold, and there's that fear of like, well, what if it falls back down to 3000, and now they can't sell for 5000 anymore? That's a big risk. And then the other thing is that there's, I think, a lot of geopolitical risk in a lot of these miners that don't do business in the US or Canada.

And so it would really, I think, come down to a lot of their individual. I know B2G (BTG) had a big deal with, Julia, what was that country that they were in? It was somewhere in West Africa.

Julia Ostian: In Mali. Yeah, they had a huge problem.

Jack Bowman: In Mali. And the government kicked them out.

Rena Sherbill: Okay. Anything else before we get into the tech side of things, which we've seen have some momentum after some? Well, there's just volatility, right? There's just volatility afoot. so I guess, what would you say about, are, Julia mentioned that Nvidia earnings are coming up next week. We also saw many of the biggest tech players report in recent weeks. We've had some nice takeaways from earnings season.

I guess, Kenio, let's start with you. What's top of mind for you when you look at tech? If you want to specify Amazon, feel free to or any other stocks. But in general, how are you looking at that part of the market?

Kenio Fontes: I feel like it's a great time for stock picking like we are not technically in a bear market because the index fell like 5 or 4 percent but some tech stocks were beating down like 20, 30, 40 % if we are talking about any video based on the all time highs. So I feel like it's a great time to go contrary and invest like in quality names, but in a cheap price. I think Amazon is my number one priority right now.

A couple quarters ago, I don't feel comfortable enough to increase my position, but right now I do because the sentiment about what market is feeling about Amazon it's just short term.

CapEx, of course, will put some pressure on the cash flow in the next one, maybe two, maybe three years. But I think the ROI will be very, very, very good, enhance the EPS, the compounding in the next decade. They invest in robots, AI chips. It's not only AWS capacity or an AI race. It makes sense. So I think we can benefit from this market euphoria.

Rena Sherbill: What would you say most concerns you when you're looking at Amazon or other tech names that you like? What has you kind of worried the most?

Kenio Fontes: Probably regulation. I mean Amazon is the company like too big to fail So I think some countries some regions may feel like We should stop Amazon.

I think this is my number one risk in the long term not of course unions and all this, things like layoffs all of this I think can impact in the long term not only AWS because I'm not concerned if they lose 1% of market share to Azure or GCP I think the main long-term risk is regulation and all of that, not the market share because it's a very, very wide-moat company.

Julia Ostian: I have actually just downgraded Amazon. It had been my top pick for a long time. Yeah, I had a strong buy and I downgraded it to buy and it was like the reaction of the market of the people that read me was not probably the nicest because everyone was like, no, but you were like, they were the one telling us that Amazon should go up.

But yeah, I actually I got pretty disappointed this time around with Amazon not people are usually concerned about the capex, right and Amazon just presented an enormous capex.

But this was not the point of why I actually decided to downgrade this stock. I really did not like the free cashflow situation of Amazon because it went down three times in the last year from Q4 2024 to Q4 2025. And it's a lot for Amazon to cover the capex that was back then. And if we take into account that Amazon wants to increase it more than 50 % from here.

I'm not sure they really understand how to cover that cost and together with this day fire people and I think they want those fire rings and this money to cover Partly the capex which definitely should not be enough.

So all of these signals and I'm just not sure and also I compare it to the return on invested capital of Amazon to other Mag 7, for example, and Amazon stands at about 15 or 16 percent, which is okay because their weighted average cost of capital is at about 10 or 11. It's okay. This company does okay. But it doesn't do that good to invest 200 billion dollars in year in CapEx, in my opinion.

So here the worry is actually not the CapEx itself, but where it actually goes and how the company will be able to pay for this. Because with other Mag 7, for example, I'm not worried. They've got the money. So even if they overbuild the capacity, they can cover it.

They can use this capacity themselves and they will be fine. But with Amazon, I don't know. I just got nervous, so I downgraded it and I'm not buying on this dip or whatever. I'm not adding to my position, although I'm not selling also.

Rena Sherbill: So you'd consider it a hold at this point?

Julia Ostian: Yeah, yeah, for now, for now I'm holding and I'm pretty happy with where I am right now. If the company continues with this, with the layoffs and with the CapEx increase, I might trim even, but not yet.

I just want to see for a couple more quarters where it goes.

Jack Bowman: The Amazon layoffs don't really surprise me because I've been watching them build an army of robots.

And I mean this literally, there was this beautiful chart that Ark put out, part of their big ideas presentation that was showing the amount of people at Amazon and the amount of robots at Amazon. And you can see that the amount of people is very slowly dwindling. It peaked in 2021, but the amount of robots is still hitting all time highs, right?

And we're getting close to where in maybe five years, there may be more robots than people at Amazon.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, they achieved like a million robots last year so that's a lot.

Jack Bowman: And so I'm seeing some of this of like, well, maybe we can just automate the entire warehouse top to bottom, right? Maybe we don't need as many people as we thought we did. That's a scary thought for the larger economy and the labor market.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah and what's the impact on margin on bottom line like triple I don't know double at least in the next 10 years?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, humans cost a lot of money, right? Way more than robots.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, it's a strange thing to think, but...

Rena Sherbill: Well, at the risk of wanting to put all of our heads under our desks in cowering fear of what's to come.

Julia, I'm interested, you mentioned that your subscribers were questioning your change in thesis on Amazon. Did it surprise you as well? Was it, did you need like a quarter or two or a couple announcements in consecutive release that forced you into a slightly more bearish stance. And then I guess also, how do you communicate that to your subscribers in terms of a change in thesis?

Julia Ostian: It's a great question because I think people or some people, not all of them, but I think people tend to fall in love with companies and it can sound weird, but people really get into the business. They really learn about the management.

They really learn about how it goes. they basically decide that they will stick in no matter what happens. So for me, it's not like that. And I'm very simple with letting it go. If I see that I don't like something, I change my opinion in a second. I don't need to wait. I don't need to double check it for a few more times.

For example, if something drastic, if something horrible happens, right? If something that I'm not sure about, like with Amazon in this case, I just, could downgrade and say that I'm cautious and this is not what I was expecting and I can just wait and see for a couple of more quarters but I think it's a big problem in general of people they want to not to analyze what's going on, they want to believe what they heard in the first place.

So I'm not surprised. I take it as what it is I just show my opinion I just explain how I view all of this market and wait and see for a couple of more quarters and my thought processing probably, my decision making, and people can just take it for whatever it is.

Rena Sherbill: And I think to your point, a lot of people's resistance, I would imagine, is just the fear of change. People do not like change. And to your point that you're easy with change and that feels like something that's not a challenge for you is probably why you're good at investing.

Julia Ostian: Probably, yeah.

I think that's like a big thing is that people are scared to change their mind. They're scared to doubt themselves like all of those just human nature things come into play and you have to be super disciplined or naturally disciplined to see that through.

Julia Ostian: Definitely, yeah.

Rena Sherbill: Jack, anything else to add on the Amazon story?

Jack Bowman: So I think that the one thing that kind of ties us all together is like the current issue with software.

Amazon is running up into this now as a model provider as well. Of this fear that software is being eaten by AI and that AI CapEx is going to eventually build out enough capacity to replace most software orgs with some sort of in-house solution or whatever and cause them all to drop off, right? Salesforce (CRM) is down like 40% off of its highs, among others. Adobe (ADBE) is getting slaughtered and a couple others. ServiceNow (NOW) is a big one.

And I have been kind of pushing back on this in some ways of like, it's bled over into Microsoft (MSFT), into Amazon. And I don't think that the fear is well placed. I think there's a lot of like AI exuberance still in the air of it can solve everything. It is an omnipresent tool that can be immensely useful forever.

And I just don't think that's the case. I think we're going to run into a wall at some point with the market of we're going to either have to produce the gains that were, you got to spend money to make money. So we do have to eventually actually make money on all of this spending.

And then also we have to prove its usefulness. Software isn't dead if AI never becomes as useful as we think it might. And I think a lot of us are kind of putting the blinders on for that trade of like software's dead and AI's king and we can forget about it, but I don't think that's true.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, and I think we have to mention the overreaction, not only about Amazon, but about whether the money is going like, Walmart is trading at 45 times earnings or 50 times earnings. Like it's a 70 % premium related to Amazon. So I think it's just too much, like the market is just so with so much fear about Amazon, but so confident about Walmart (WMT). it's both good companies, but t's just too different, you know?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, one's not covering the planet in data centers.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, it's inflation linked but they are growing in the low single digits. So how can you trade it like 50 times earnings? Come on.

Rena Sherbill: Kenio, do you have a price target on Amazon or do you do price targets?

Kenio Fontes: I don't do much about price target, it's much more based on margin of safety. So I like to buy Amazon in 200s, 220s I think it's fine too, it can achieve an IRR of double digits in my models. But that's it, the lower the better. Of course, if I consider everything will be okay with Amazon, the lower the better.

Rena Sherbill: Any earning season takeaways, really from anyone, but specifically in the tech space? Kenio, I'll stick with you for a second. Anything that you took away from this recent earning season?

Kenio Fontes: Yeah about capex it stand out to me. Apple (AAPL) is a different one. I think we all have something to comment about Apple it's just i think it's strange how Apple is sticking to its cash flow like I need this cash flow, I need to buy back and on the other hand why Apple almost is showing to me like they'd have nothing to invest on. Nothing big - they're not developing some huge chips, some self-driving cars like Amazon and Alphabet is doing.

They're not on a data center business, they're not on the LLM business, they're on the buyback business so I would like to see Apple depending less on this engineering, but at the same time I think - what does Apple management what is Tim Cook seeing that I'm not seeing. Is he seeing it's all FOMO.

I'm not participating on this AI race, nothing related to LLM or nothing. I'm sticking to my buybacks with the 3 % shareholder yield and that's it. So I feel like it's a good discussion.

Jack Bowman: Apple has this genius strategy, right? They reduced their capex by 20 % last year. No need to spend on data centers, everyone else will do it for us.

Then they enter this deal with Google, right? A billion dollars a year to lease Gemini to replace Siri's thoughts. And Google's spending way more than a billion dollars a year to run Gemini. So I know that Apple's getting their money's worth, right? Spend no money, reap all the reward, pay a little bit later, pay some service charges, and it's working out for them so far.

Julia Ostian: Yeah, and also if we think about this in this way, if for example, AI proves to be a woman bust and bust afterwards, right? If we all wake up one day and realize that it was all for nothing, like the Metaverse, although I don't think it can be.

But in general, if that happens, Apple has a very, very high chance being the strongest stock in the market and the stock where everyone will run and the software everyone will run.

And in general, if you want to diversify away from AI, you have a Mag 7 that basically has no exposure to this whatsoever. You can look at it this way. Although I don't buy Apple stuff myself.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah. I don't like Apple either. I mean, I like the company, but I don't like the valuation.

Julia Ostian: Yeah, I really like the company. Everything here is Apple. Like if you come to my apartment, everything, everything. But I don't buy the stock.

Kenio Fontes: Yes, I have an iPhone and Apple watch, everything, but i don't don't like the stock.

Rena Sherbill: I remember reading an article in the New Yorker a bunch of years ago about a private school in New York and the kids were like, Apple owns us. Apple owns all of us. Just everybody has Apple products.

Julia, anything you would add to the earnings takeaway conversation from the tech space?

Julia Ostian: Going into Nvidia's earnings. I think it will be really interesting. The market looks nervous and I mean the market looks excited but worried at the same time which basically surprises me because I don't know with Nvidia I guess from what I've started I just broke down Nvidia's earnings today I wrote the preview so I have a fresh thoughts on this.

It really looks like doesn't matter what Nvidia will do. The market will not be as happy because it looks to me that the company so many times have beaten the expectations that basically right now the expectations is that everything will be better than expected.

But the problem is that this phrase can stretch basically limitless, right? What is better than expected? Anything can be better than expected. It can be slightly better than expected but if Nvidia will deliver instead of $65 billion, $70 billion, I'm not sure the market will be very happy because Nvidia usually beats like three to four billion.

I'm just curious to see what will happen because the market is down a couple of percentage points one day. And then everyone is excited about what the Fed will say about the rate cuts like today, for example, on Wednesday, and the market is flying. I don't know, I'm just curious what will happen. And I'm sure Nvidia by the way will post a great quarter and I think the whole year looks really good for Nvidia right now with the capex spending, most of the capex spending will go to Nvidia one way or another.

So I don't know. I'm just curious to see what will happen. And I really think the stock can go lower on the earnings even with the bid.

Kenio Fontes: I kind of agree with Julia and I think that in the outlook for the next quarter it may be better than the market expects because of this huge capex. I think in the short-term in 2026, 2027, maybe 2030 it will be very bullish for Nvidia because Amazon is trying to reduce this dependence on Nvidia chips, same for Google.

It's not that short term, they can't just produce a new chip to their cloud business in three or five years, so I think it's still bullish in the short term, but in the long term we will have a little bit more uncertainty.

Rena Sherbill: Jack, anything to add to this part of the conversation?

Jack Bowman: Nvidia is always an interesting business to me because they have this such high profit margin, it's like 70 % on their stuff, because they don't actually manufacture their own chips, right? It's TSMC (TSM) does the actual making of the chips.

Nvidia designs them. And then they sell the completed units once they've paid TSMC to actually manufacture them. So this leaves them in a space where improving the product doesn't necessarily always like hurt them in the cost to make it, because the manufacturer shares some of that cost which allows Nvidia to have these times where it's like they spend all this money on R &D.

They were down last quarter a little bit because of this crazy R &D spending on this new chip that they had to produce. It takes a lot of money to get it up and running.

Now we've had a quarter of it running. And I think now we're starting to see Nvidia turn back into the cash machine. So I'm also bullish going in. I see them beating.

The Rubin model chip has been doing very well in its deployment in data centers, as limited as it is from what I can hear.

Rena Sherbill: What else would we add to the tech part of the conversation, AI or tech adjacent or tech related? Any other insights?

Jack Bowman: I think there's a consideration that investors should have in the current tech space in very much splitting hardware and software. And even among people like Nvidia, is by all means people say a hardware company.

But even splitting between designers and manufacturers, that we're starting to see this dichotomy between software and hardware. I expect us to see a continued dichotomy between producers and designers as well. I'm not as bullish on producers of chips as much as I am on designers of chips, like AMD, Nvidia.

Maybe not so much Intel (INTC). I just sold my Intel shares a little bit ago.

Rena Sherbill: Why is that?

Jack Bowman: So, well, I bought them in the government dip. When the government bought the Intel, everyone says rock bottom, I bought shares. So I was up 150 % over six months.

And I looked at the valuation, I looked at the fact that nothing had changed. The CEO in his earnings call kept saying, well, there's this crazy demand, there's CPU demand, we can't fill it all because we just have so many people on our chips. And then they immediately reported a lower than expected sales figure.

And I was like that's a big red flag to me. Like saying we have unlimited demand and then not being able to show me that you're meeting unlimited demand is not a good sign for me so I left.

But I also had a crazy trade of like six months I was up tremendously in the position and I thought too much risk now on the table.

Rena Sherbill: Last week I talked to Tech Contrarians and we were talking about AMD and Nvidia and there were lot of comments about the pairing of AMD and Nvidia going forward. Anything that y'all would say about (AMD)?

Julia Ostian: I really like AMD, I have this in the stock in my portfolio. I don't know, I'm holding. I think this market is huge. think going forward, this market will definitely have the space for not only two players, but more players. And actually, NVIDIA's mode is not only the designing of the chips, it's mostly the ecosystem which companies cannot simply turn away from. It's the CUDA that it has and everything basically all of the infrastructure has been built on this.

So it's not easy just to produce as for example for hyperscalers the new chip and just adopt it and that's it and we move on from Nvidia it's just it's impossible so Nvidia will stay and I think there is definitely place for AMD on this market because as we've seen Nvidia sometimes he's the cycles where it simply cannot satisfy the demand that there is.

And in those exact situations, I think AMD could be nice to cover for maybe smaller companies, maybe some other segments of the business, maybe not hyperscalers, but I see definitely space for AMD there.

And I myself hold for pretty long time, I bought the share of this company at a pretty cheap price, I think it went down to like 70 or $80 per share. So I'm just happy to hold it. I don't know, don't see a big threat for AMD here.

I think this company will slowly capture the market share. Of course, it's not growing as Nvidia does, but at the same time, I'm not sure it should be growing in the same way. It doesn't have the same infrastructure and so on.

Kenio Fontes: I have a few AMD too, but I have some concerns about the company, mainly in the short term. As Julia said, it's not like Nvidia, it's not the same infrastructure, same ecosystem and all, but I think 2026 will be the year that the AMD has to prove itself.

It has to capture demand. It is growing, but it needs to grow more, mainly in the data center revenue. They need to increase their margins and prove okay we have some pricing power not like Nvidia has but we also have some pricing power so and also about SBC the stock based compensation is just too high so we see it's 30 times earnings forward but with if we consider the SBC is 40 or something near that so i think it have AMD have to prove itself in 2026.

I think it's a good company with good prospects too, but they have to prove this demand, the pricing power, and also that they can have this operating leverage with the SBC, growing less in revenue.

Rena Sherbill: Jack, anything you want to say about the FOMC minutes coming out today or macro speaking? Anything of note?

Jack Bowman: The Fed is in this really weird limbo right now. And I think the minutes are gonna show us a divided Fed, one that is more divided than normal, but is still leaning toward the institutional, we should wait and see, and rate cuts take a long time to filter into the economy.

We did a couple cuts and now we're gonna sit and wait. And they're still in agreement with that philosophy, but it's changing. We're introducing more more participants like Governor Moran, who really wants rate cuts and every time they vote for a cut, he votes for 50 points and every time they vote for a whole, he votes for a cut.

And so we're seeing more people agree with him and take his stance that we should be lower than where we are now. I don't think they're gonna lower until June, but a lot of it has to do with where the macro goes in terms of like unemployment.

Unemployment's peaked. It was at 4.6%. Now we're down to 4.3. But if it comes back up again, that's a threat to the economy. So the Fed will have to act sooner. I don't think we'll see anything until June. I think we're going to have to sit and wait to get any action until then.

Rena Sherbill: Appreciate that. What would you guys want to get into right now? What do you think is most deserving of attention these days?

Jack Bowman: I think to Kenio's point earlier, and just to reiterate this to people, we are in a stock picker's market. The year to date, the average stock in the S &P has outperformed the S &P, which is rare in our markets that are just mag seven driven. The last 17 years, that has just not been true for the most part, most of the time. And that's true right now.

So right now I think there's a lot of opportunities in some of these peaking down sectors that are now getting some love. But to be cautious, because I don't think, I still don't think, it's a full rotation forever. Like I don't think growth is dead and value is the new driver of the market.

But in the meantime, while the market's still figuring out this AI deal, while we're all still on the cusp about what the Fed's been doing after forever, there's some opportunity. Like, chemicals have been going crazy. And I was never an investor in Dow or anything like that.

But recently, I've been looking at these charts saying these look much better than any of the time that I've ever been an investor.

Rena Sherbill: What are you looking at in the charts specifically?

Kenio Fontes: I think a better quote would be by Peter Lynch, buy business you understand, not you like, but at least you understand. I think it's better.

Rena Sherbill: I'm going to leave everybody with the final word and where everybody can find your insight, your analysis, perhaps your YouTube videos. Jack, I'll start with you since we just finished with you. Happy for you to share that with our audience.

Jack Bowman: Yeah, run a, so I don't upload much to YouTube, but I run a newsletter every week called The Macro Obsession. And for followers or those that want to ask questions, feel free to leave us a comment. We hope to have all three back on at least once a quarter. So look forward to that. data.title: Figma: Strong Results, Topping Expectations
data.description:
Web-based design and product development platform Figma, Inc. (FIG), released better-than-expected Q4 results, and investors are taking notice.

The company went public in mid-2025 to much anticipation and excitement. Priced at $33/share, FIG stock soared on the IPO date to the mid-$130/share range. The stock has since fallen back to earth, with shares now trading below its IPO price.

For its first public fiscal year, the company reported a loss with positive cash flow while maintaining a healthy balance sheet. FIG has mixed sentiment among the analyst community, and I tend to lean more neutral but with a bullish tilt, especially this early on in its public tenure. At the very least, I believe the stock can reclaim its IPO price, and I believe the results justify this move.

FIG Stock Metrics

Consensus EPS estimates for FIG currently are $0.22/share for 2026 and $0.52/share for fiscal 2029.

At both ends, the stock appears very richly valued. At today’s prices, FIG is over 100x its near-term earnings estimate and over 40x the longer-range estimate. I would find it hard to invest at these prices unless I had a high degree of confidence in the future outlook.

The broader Seeking Alpha (“SA”) community is similarly neutral. Wall Street analysts, however, are bullish, though coverage is relatively light. For those covering the stock, consensus price targets range from a low of $30/share to a high of nearly $100/share.

Can the stock reach these targets? Possibly over time, especially the low end of the range. There is little room for error with results, however, and that could create a steep mountain for FIG. That said, it appears FIG is outperforming on that front.

FIG Operating Performance

If there is one clear takeaway from the quarter, it’s that FIG is executing at a very high level.

Topline revenues in Q4, for example, were up 40% YOY and ahead of guidance. For the full year, revenue crossed the +$1B mark, growing 41%. Importantly, growth actually accelerated into Q4, which is not something you typically see from companies at this scale.

Retention and expansion metrics were equally impressive. Net dollar retention for customers spending more than +$10K in ARR jumped to 136%, up five points sequentially and the highest level in over two years.

In addition, gross retention held steady at 97%. This signals to me durability in the base customers. And among larger customers, the population continues to ramp meaningfully, with the +$100K+ ARR cohort growing 46% YOY and +$1M+ ARR customers up 68%.

Margins remain healthy as well. Gross margins held in the high-80% range despite surging AI usage, and the company delivered a 14% operating margin in Q4 with positive free cash flow. While investment is increasing, particularly in AI, the underlying business model looks strong, in my view.

Based on results, I believe the demand signals appear valid. The question now becomes how much of that strength is already priced into the stock at current trading levels.

What Is The Outlook For FIG Stock?

Looking ahead, management is clearly leaning into the momentum from their first half-year of being a public company.

For Q1 2026, FIG is guiding to revenue between +$315M and $317M. This would imply roughly 38% growth. For the full year, guidance calls for nearly +$1.4B in revenue, or about 30% growth at the midpoint. That’s a step down from 2025’s 41% pace, but still very strong by most standards.

What stands out to me is that the outlook assumes continued seat expansion, international growth, and increasing AI adoption. Roughly 75% of paid customers with more than +$10K in ARR are already consuming AI credits weekly, and beginning in March, FIG will monetize both seats and credits. That shift introduces upside potential but also some modeling uncertainty, in my view.

On a more negative note, margins are expected to compress, with full-year operating margin guided to around 8% as investment in AI accelerates. That tells me management is prioritizing long-term positioning over near-term profitability.

Analysts appear to be positive on the outlook but are more measured on valuation, with both RBC and J.P. Morgan maintaining neutral-equivalent ratings while trimming price targets amid multiple compression across software.

If FIG continues to execute and AI monetization ramps smoothly, the targets look achievable. But expectations remain high, and there is little room for missteps in this environment, in my view.

Is FIG Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

After walking through the numbers and the outlook, I find myself incrementally more bullish.

The valuation is not cheap by any traditional metric. Trading at over 100x near-term earnings and more than 40x longer-range estimates is a premium multiple, no doubt. But premium multiples are often reserved for premium growth, and right now, FIG is delivering.

Revenue growth accelerated to 40% in Q4. Net dollar retention hit 136%. Large customers are scaling. AI adoption is already embedded across the base, with monetization of credits just beginning. These are not signs of a company losing relevance in an AI-driven world; they are signs of one leaning into it, in my view.

Yes, operating margins are set to compress as investment ramps. Yes, expectations are high. And yes, there is little room for execution missteps. But the stock is down more than 30% YTD and trading below its IPO price of $33/share. Based on the current trajectory, I believe reclaiming that IPO level is reasonable and justified by the results and forward guidance.

For investors comfortable with volatility and long-term execution risk, I believe FIG is a worthy speculative flyer for those seeking outsized medium- to long-term gains.

data.title: The Cheesecake Factory: A Sweet Treat For Shareholders
data.description:
We view The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated (CAKE) stock as a Hold here following a nice rally from the fall of 2025 lows. The valuation and growth combination suggests the stock is about fairly valued for the level of growth. That said, as far as restaurants are concerned, The Cheesecake Factory is delivering stable growth, which is difficult in this competitive space. In this column, we discuss the just-reported quarter from our long-term house position in this stock. We particularly like that not only is there slow and stable growth, but there is a growing dividend and share repurchases.

The Cheesecake Factory Revenue Comparable Same Store Sales

Whenever we analyze the performance of a company in the restaurant space, comparable sales are the key metric we hone in on, followed by the overall growth in revenues. For Cheesecake Factory in Q4, revenues once again rose in Q4 vs. the prior year's Q4. The Cheesecake Factory delivered a top-line sales figure of $961.6 million, up 4.4% year-over-year. This beat consensus estimates by $12.7 million, as sales were expected to be closer to flat. However, that came with comparable sales results that were down 2.2% from last year's quarter. How did revenues rise if comps fell? Well, the company had about 7% unit growth from a year ago, so the revenue was influenced by more sales, including the seven new restaurants that opened up in Q4. How about margin power?

The Cheesecake Factory Margins

So first, food and beverage costs were 21.6% of sales, down from the 22.3% of last year, which was a 70-basis-point improvement. Further, labor expenses were just 33.9% of sales, which was down from 34.2% last year, an additional 40-basis-point improvement. We like this improvement as it translates to better margins. However, general and administrative expenses were 7.0% of overall revenues, rising from 6.3% last year, but other operating expenses and costs were 20 basis points lower than a year ago. But some lease expenses and preopening costs drove overall margin lower. There was a 160 basis point softening in operating margin to 3.5% from 5.1% last year.

CEO David Overton commented on the strong performance:

We delivered solid fourth quarter and full-year results in 2025, generating record annual revenue supported by 25 new restaurant openings for the year...despite a more challenging operating environment across the restaurant industry, including weather-related impacts, revenue for the quarter finished within our expected range. Margins and adjusted diluted net income per share finished toward the higher end of our expectations.

So a record quarter for revenues is always welcomed, and despite the overall operating margin decline, it was better than management had expected. The sales print, as we saw, was helped by more openings. The company plans to open up to 26 new restaurants in 2026 across its several banner names. Margin power is being well-controlled as far as labor and food costs are concerned, so we will have to see if the lease expense and opening-related expenses can be controlled and drive earnings even higher.

The Cheesecake Factory Earnings Power

The Q4 results showed declining comparable sales, margins that narrowed but accretive unit growth, and a better-than-expected overall top line. Putting together revenue and expenses, we saw Q4 adjusted EPS of $1.00, which was essentially in line (a one-penny beat). For the year, there was 10% EPS growth to $3.77 from $3.44. Assuming 8-10% EPS growth is possible for 2026, it would suggest that at today's share price of $65, shares are valued appropriately at 15-16X FWD EPS.

The Cheesecake Factory Raises The Dividend

Another reason we are happy to hold a house position is the growing dividend. In fact, the company just announced an 11.1% increase to the quarterly dividend, so it now pays $0.30 quarterly. While the yield is about 2%, which is not large by any stretch, over the long term, dividend growth investing is a winning play, something we love at BAD BEAT Investing. But it was the first raise in 15 quarters, so it would be better, of course, to get annual increases.

That said, along with the dividend hike, last week the board approved an increase of 5.0 million shares to the existing repurchase program, raising the total authorization to 66 million shares, which means about 6 million shares can be repurchased still. In Q4 another 228,100 shares were retired. So this boosts EPS power and shareholder value.

Forward View

We like holding here, and rate shares a market perform at these levels. New money could pounce if there is another wave lower in shares to the mid-$50s, as the risk-reward would be better there given the slow and stable growth. We think 8-10% EPS growth is possible on the high side, though consensus is for $4.03 in 2026, which is 6.9% growth. So overall, we think it is a Hold here, collect the dividend, and let the company slowly grow.

data.title: GPIX Vs. ISPY: Why Goldman's Dynamic Strategy Beats 0DTE On Total Return
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Given my expectations for the S&P 500 to continue its upward trend in 2026, I believe that an asset such as the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) has an advantage over the ProShares S&P 500 High Income ETF (ISPY). While they share a common goal, their investment strategies differ significantly in their implementation methods. Based on my forecast, I believe that by actively using options to cover only part of the portfolio (from 25% to 75%) and physically owning shares, GPIX's structure allows long-term investors to capture most of the market rally. It is an excellent tool for total return investors who are not willing to sacrifice capital value for high monthly payments. The ISPY, meanwhile, applies daily options (0DTE/1DTE), whose active use cuts off a larger portion of growth potential. The S&P 500 is expected to grow in 2026, enabling GPIX to provide investors with 3% to 5% more total return than ISPY.

Fundamental Similarities Between GPIX and ISPY

Perhaps the most important similarity between GPIX and ISPY is their focus on generating high monthly income while also providing capital growth. The following funds share a number of fundamental similarities:

  • Their underlying asset is the S&P 500 index, so they're pretty diversified in terms of both the number of holdings and sectors.
  • Neither fund aims to balance high returns with capital growth. This means that profits are generated without eroding capital, a definite advantage over many other ETFs that actively use option strategies.
  • Call options are the main instrument in the funds' strategy; this automatically limits profit growth during aggressive market growth; however, the size of the cap itself varies.
  • Under their dividend policy, each fund pays dividends monthly. The yield is also roughly the same (8.07% and 8.26%), rendering them mutually exclusive assets for many investors.

Fundamental Differences Between GPIX and ISPY

More in-depth analysis of ETFs allows us to identify the criteria by which they should be distinguished. This first and most important criterion relates to their portfolio composition. A GPIX is a traditional direct ownership fund, within the framework of which physical purchases are made of shares in issuers included in the S&P 500 index. Through direct share ownership, the GPIX provides a more direct correlation with the index and minimizes counterparty risks.

Even though the ISPY portfolio has the same wide range of assets, the portfolio isn't built by actually owning the stocks; it's done by creating a synthetic replica. Managers of the fund invest in S&P 500 issuers through the purchase of derivative instruments and invest in treasury bills for the purpose of forming margin collateral. ISPY's goal is to track the S&P 500 Daily Covered Call Index as closely as possible, not just to own stocks. The use of synthetic replication by ISPY frees up capital for treasury bills.

Besides portfolio composition, their differences in how they use options are important, and this affects the growth ceiling. Those managing GPIX use a dynamic hedging strategy, with options covering between 25% and 75% of the total portfolio. In the event that the fund anticipates a continuation of the bull market, it may utilize a lower percentage of hedging, allowing for the potential to participate in market growth. On the contrary, it is expected that the market may undergo a correction, and the options coverage is maximum, allowing for softening losses in overall profitability within the limits of the premium received. Managers of ISPY employ a different approach—a systematic one, in which new "at-the-money" call options with a one-trading-day expiration (0DTE) are sold every day. This means that each day starts with a reset of previous option trades, minimizing the risk associated with options losing value due to rapid growth at the beginning of the month.

Option strategies also affect how income is generated, where the GPIX strategy involves combining returns for investors (based on base dividends and option premiums), whereas ISPY generates income from daily premiums. Option types 0DTE make ISPY's income generation mechanics more aggressive, whilst GPIX is capable of generating income more calmly. Also, check out the differences between GPIX and ISPY when it comes to risk management and tax efficiency. To give you an example, a key risk for GPIX is the human factor, because the results of a flexible approach to option coverage depend on how skilled the managers are. For ISPY, the main risk is tied to how 0DTE options are used, as this option strategy increases the risks linked to sharp intraday crashes.

From a tax efficiency perspective, in my opinion, there is an advantage to GPIX, as its dividends over the past year have been 88% to 90% Return of Capital on average. As for ISPY, 89% of its latest dividend payments also consisted of Return of Capital. Due to the use of swaps and index options, the fund is theoretically eligible for Section 1256 status. In reality, there is no stable payout structure, because from month to month, investors receive different shares of ROC in the 19a-1 payout structure. Summing up the data for the last year, about 75% of ISPY's dividends are paid as ROC.

Given that GPIX returns are characterized by the specific taxation of options (Section 1256), the return of capital received by investors does not erode NAV. That makes it possible to defer tax payments until the fund shares are sold, which takes away concerns about NAV declines. The return of capital for ISPY is less stable and has a more complex tax structure due to the use of synthetics and daily trading. For this reason, there is less predictability in the payment structure for ISPY compared to GPIX. That said, ROC for ISPY is also constructive and does not lead to NAV erosion. This is confirmed, in the first place, since the launch of the fund, its Total Return has been above the level of distributable dividends. Furthermore, some of the distributions are classified as ROC due to the fact that the use of 0DTE options creates tax complications as seen on Form 1099.

GPIX and ISPY Comparison Table

A comparison table based on key criteria is provided below to help you determine the difference between GPIX and ISPY. The table shows both qualitative and quantitative indicators.
