When comparing ETFs, my primary focus is on Total Return values. With GPIX and ISPY, I'm looking at their performance over the last two years, as they started in October 2023 and December 2023, accordingly. The Total Return of GPIX and ISPY had a very high correlation over a long period of time until the market crash in April 2025. The recovery process since then has been characterized by GPIX having a stable advantage of around 3% to 5%. As of now, total 2-year returns for GPIX are 4.15% higher, showing the fund's actual performance during a bull market. Of course, this assumes reinvestment of distributions. This means that while both funds show the same NAV decline during a market downturn, in a bull market, and especially when growth is aggressive, the advantage lies with GPIX. This can be seen as proof of a higher growth ceiling than ISPY.

Risks of the Investment Thesis

The analysis of GPIX and ISPY has demonstrated their fundamental differences, due to which I would like to analyze the risks separately for each fund. This is due to the fact that they have different planning horizons and methods of replicating the S&P 500 index.

Beginning with GPIX, it's worth noting that their risks are mainly related to the human factor. The active management of the portfolio implies that the decisions of portfolio managers have a significant impact on the actual results of the fund. It is especially evident in the decision-making process regarding what portion of the portfolio is covered by options. The risk associated with high concentration in the technology sector is also worth noting. Around 33.5% of the GPIX portfolio consists of stocks linked to technological growth, rendering it highly sensitive to any correction related to the AI supercycle.

Lastly, the potential for long-term growth to be limited is worth mentioning, because using weekly or monthly options could cause stock prices to go above the strike price if there's a sharp rally. If that happens, it could lead to losses from the options and limit NAV growth.

When it comes to ISPY, the main risks of this fund are tied to how daily options work and how volatile they are. With 0DTE options, you've got this specific Gamma risk. This is all about how sensitive the option's delta is to changes in the price of the underlying asset, and it can be at its highest right when the option expires. As a result, sharp price changes in the last hour before the market closes can make an option position unprofitable. These risks are also linked to intraday volatility, because a one-sided market decline during a single trading session can lead to a loss-making day. Moreover, the 0DTE premium only slightly covers the negative result. Furthermore, when the market rises sharply the next morning, it is not possible for the fund to keep up with it due to the new call sold.

Conclusion

Direct comparison of GPIX and ISPY funds suggests that in 2026, if the bull market on the S&P 500 continues, Total Returns on GPIX will likely exceed those on ISPY. Even though my recommendation for both funds is "Buy," my preference is for GPIX. When using a dynamic options strategy and the ability to reduce portfolio coverage to 25%, higher growth ceilings are ensured, having a positive effect on monthly payments to investors. In a bear market, I expect GPIX to have an advantage because portfolio coverage of up to 75% allows for more active capital protection than ISPY's systematic approach to derivatives. Moreover, the GPIX fund's other advantages, which include a higher historical Total Return, lower expense ratio (0.29% vs. 0.56%), and tax transparency and efficiency in the form of a capital return of approximately 90% of the total dividend amount, contribute to my preference for it.

data.title: Emera: Decent Returns, But The Risk For 2026 Is Now Higher
data.description:
Please note that dollar amounts in this article are CAD, Canadian dollars, unless otherwise noted.

In my last article on Emera Incorporated (EMA), I gave the company a neutral rating of "HOLD" due to what I considered to be an unattractive risk/reward proposition. This wasn't, and isn't necessarily, a reflection of poor fundamentals or poor company appeal, but rather a reflection of the broader uninvestability of the utility sector. I have covered this broadly over the past half-year or so, and recent coverage also includes massive multi-utilities like Southern (SO). In my view, very few utility stocks are spared this significant valuation inflation.

As a whole, I don't view it as valid. It's affected by things like significant CapEx plans related to grid and infrastructure expansion, where returns for those investments are relatively uncertain. It's also related to higher service and revenue volume expectations from companies involved in data centers and the like. I also view this as uncertain.

The investor enthusiasm regarding this power demand, driven largely by expectations of AI, is fairly well-established. It has turned what used to be conservative, low-yield (most often) investments focused not on massive returns and growth but instead on income into sentiment-driven plays. There's inherent danger with such expectations and such a thesis.

It would, of course, be too much to claim that we've seen this sentiment drop now - but we've seen movements in many of these companies, with some already tapering off or even declining. Emera, the focus of today's article, has done fairly well, all things considered - it hasn't significantly inflated further, and it's still outperformed the broader market, at least slightly.

How does that influence the company's near-term potential? That's what we're going to look at in this article and try to determine for 2026E. To help us with this determination, we have the company's recent results, we have upcoming earnings, and we have sector-specific trends, which I alluded to above. Both of these things, together with the company valuation, will determine the appeal of the company at this price.

Emera: Unappealing Given Broader Sector Trends

The reason that I don't regret not buying Emera despite market outperformance is due to the relative performance of the actual utility picks I made - among others, National Grid (NGG) and SSE PLC (SSEZY), which both have outperformed the sector as well as Emera. That is not to say that Emera is a bad choice - anytime you're outperforming the market, you can consider yourself having done a "good job". It's how I measure success as an investor. Have I beaten the market or not? If I do it consistently, that's usually enough to set me apart from other analysts and investors in a positive way - and quickly.

The company's performance has good reason. The underlying trends, not only for the past quarter but also since my last article in mid-2025, have been very solid. The rate expansion that I theorized about in my last article, specific to Florida, seems to have materialized. It's fair to say that the company performed above market expectations, with earnings growth close to double digits. The Florida CapEx focus is changing the company's operational profile from a Nova Scotia/Canada/Caribbean-focused utility to something more of a US-focused utility. This has both drawbacks and advantages, which I believe will become clearer with time.

First off, and this is a bit of a complexity that I covered somewhat - FX risk. Emera is Canadian, but now its Florida earnings will come in USD. The USD has not been performing well - nor do I believe it will perform well in the near term. I also covered in my last article the non-trivial execution risk related to the company's Florida operations. There's a high amount of uncertainty there - cost overrun potential and less than constructive forward regulator negotiations are both realistic potentials.

Furthermore, and this hardly needs any proof, Florida is probably, together with California, one of the most climate-impacted areas in all of the United States. Building and working here, expanding and establishing, needs to at the very least be discounted or considered relative to this. Also, don't expect a whole lot of dividend growth - the company has already cited this to be at no more than 1-2%, even if target AEPS growth is as high as 7%. That 7% is also the current typical average for most of the utilities I cover. It seems to be close to an industry benchmark insofar as expectations, accounting for AI-driven growth, seem to go.

Fundamentals are not Emera's problem, nor do I expect them to be. Payout also seems fairly safe, and some growth seems to be likely here.

The one issue I'd point to here for the Florida geography, aside from climate and the size of investment, is demographic risks. Florida is one of the nation's youngest states, but with a high degree of young adults leaving the state due to cost-of-living issues. Is Florida a state that's attractive right now but might not be 10 years from now due to demographic changes? Time will tell - but don't believe it to be enough to mark the company down.

Overall, the combination of excellent operational efficiency (significant OCF increase) coupled with solid capital deployment (on track to meet its 2025 target, as long as 4Q doesn't present any surprises in a few days) means that you "get what you see" or "see what you get". What we have is a utility at a relatively high price point relative to its historical risk and volatility. To say that the uncertainty is gone from Emera would be going a step too far. The company still has to close New Mexico Gas, and the substantial amount of divestment and change is typically not something I like to see from a utility business.

Emera hasn't always been a positive or stable business. Despite recovery, the 5-year return trends show us a 7-8% decline for Emera, which reflects a high degree of overall volatility. The company has had an above-average debt load, which has increased its volatility due to interest rate shifts. The company's significant volatility was a result, among other things, of the TECO energy acquisition, which came with NA market exposure at the cost of significant debt. The company's shift towards Florida has made investors view Emera as a bit of a "safer" investment, more like traditional utilities.

As of 3Q25, and going into 4Q25, I have no issue with seeing Emera in this fashion - meaning I continue to value the company similar to other utility businesses.

Valuation For Emera: Upside Is Tricky

At the price we're looking at for Emera, the company has no specific dividend advantage, valuation advantage, or growth advantage beyond 2025E, as I see it. While 2025E is likely to be a significantly positive year, with 8-10% total AEPS growth, I view this as already included in the current share price. The reason that I say this is that beyond 2025, we have relatively low expectations of AEPS increasing until 2028E, less than 3-4% per year on average.

So, relative to this, the company's fundamentals do not really matter insofar as they would justify a higher valuation. The company's fundamentals do justify a good valuation - but that's my point, that we already have this valuation here.

As you can see implied above, the current valuation and upside would not create an annualized profit above 15% annualized - instead, we risk a 15x P/E negative rate of return, even with that 4%+ yield.

Of course, Emera might not be trading at 15x P/E in the long term. My previous target levels were at CAD$50/share. That means that based on a current trading level of almost $70/share, we have an overvaluation above 15% in total. The company's 20-year average is about 18x, which is in line with some utilities but not with others. I don't have an issue forecasting the company's FV at a 17-18x P/E (which is also why I have a fair value target of $65/share, the midpoint).

The issue I have is that at $70/share in terms of price, we'd need a 24-25x P/E for Emera to see an annualized rate of return of 15% per year - at least under the current forecasts. With regards and reference to the company's significant investment plan, I view the forward volatility as too high to view these estimates as higher, at least with any amount of conviction.

Utilities are expensive. This goes not just for Emera, but for many other utilities here. That's why I've sold most of the positions that I've owned, including very large positions like Enel (ENLAY), but also smaller positions as well.

I do not view Emera's 4Q25 results as materially changing the forecast. The estimate is already positive for the year, but the question isn't what happens in 2025, but what happens in the coming few quarters and between 2026 and 2028.

It essentially hinges on whether you believe the company will perform better than the current estimates. If you do not believe this, then you either share my estimate or are more bearish than me. In that scenario, it would be wise to consider not buying more or staying at a "HOLD".

If you, on the other hand, expect the company to do substantially better than what I'm saying here, or what the market is saying (I agree with the market forecasts in this case, by which I mean the analyst average), then it could be a scenario where you could see upside.

Please note, however, that this requires the company to either trade substantially higher in P/E or have substantially higher AEPS growth, and this is something I don't expect the company to really manage.

That is why the risks for the company as of 2026 are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

As of 2026, you could technically argue for Emera potentially outperforming due to very good returns from Florida - but countering this, I would continue to argue that any returns from Florida, or from any other geography for that matter, will continue to be impacted by the company's $20B capital plan, about 10x the proceeds it got from two significant sales.

This capital plan, I maintain, significantly increases regulatory risk. Furthermore, while we're currently in a declining interest rate environment (positive, lower financing costs), a rising rate would once again increase financing costs, which makes an overvalued utility like Emera a risk because investors might simply move cash to different savings. If I could get 4-5% risk-free, as I could a year ago, I certainly would pick that over Emera.

Because of this, at the 2026E time in February, and just a short time before 4Q, I view Emera as not a compelling purchase, regardless of 2025E.

Thesis

Emera is a potentially attractive, appealing company with more upside than it had when it was focused on non-regulated or different operations - at least it has more upside at the right price, which unfortunately is not the case here. Instead, I believe the current valuation for Emera reflects an over-exuberance from the company's new focus, and there are more attractive investments available on the market compared to Emera, with better yields and better conservative upside - if not necessarily in the utility sector.

I reiterate a "HOLD" here. I would price Emera at a fair value of CAD$65 for the native ticker for 2026E, unchanged from 6 months ago, which comes to a PT of no higher than CAD$50/share, also unchanged, making the company at least 20% overvalued here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of CAD$50/share for the native ticker, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of February 2026.

data.title: AI Will Completely Transform Amazon
data.description:
Over the last few years, I have written a number of articles on Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), the leading consumer marketplace and cloud provider on the market today.

In all of my coverage, I’ve been universally positive on the company, arguing that the firm’s strong moat, robust margins, considerable growth opportunity, and reasonable valuation make it a Buy:

Amazon has considerable exposure to current AI trends, owning the largest cloud business in the world, AWS. At the same time, the firm’s global e-commerce expansion is continuing apace, as the company outscales local competition, drives incredible customer value through Prime subscriptions, and layers in high-margin advertising on top.

Things have been churning away, and right now Amazon is positioned to deliver mid- to high-teens EPS growth through the end of the decade.

But what if there was something that could make the company considerably more efficient? What if Amazon could materially reduce operating expenses while maintaining (and even growing) its e-commerce volumes? What if Amazon was on the verge of unlocking substantial operating leverage, even as it scales past $1 trillion in revenue?

While considerable attention has already been paid to the impact that AI will have on Amazon’s cloud business, in this article, I wanted to take a closer look at the impact that AI might have on Amazon’s retail business. With more than 1 million employees working in Amazon warehouses, headcount is a substantial cost center for the company. Between AI optimizations and wholesale robotic replacement, what are the base and bull cases for efficiencies in Amazon's logistics network going forward?

Today, I will take a deeper look at Amazon’s current financial state, examine the company’s opportunities to transform the business, and make the case that shares look materially undervalued versus where the company could be in just a few short years.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Amazon's Current Position

Before diving into the impact that AI might have on Amazon’s core cost centers, it’s probably worth laying out exactly where Amazon currently sits and how much of the cost base is up for optimization.

Over the last year, Amazon produced $716 billion in total revenues. The company’s cost of revenues was $356 billion, leaving a gross profit of $360 billion; that implies gross margins of roughly 50%.

From there, the company has a number of operating expenses, including fulfillment, tech and infrastructure, sales, marketing, admin, and more.

Once you subtract those costs out, Amazon produced nearly $80 billion in operating income, with an operating margin of roughly 11.2%:

On a market cap of $2.21 trillion, this reflects a price-to-operating income multiple of roughly 27.6x.

You can see these numbers in the middle of the December 2025 column above.

If you dig into the company’s filings, what you’ll find is that Amazon spends roughly $103 billion on shipping costs, which it includes in its "cost of revenues" figure, and roughly $109 billion in fulfillment costs, which make up the majority of the firm's "SG&A expenses."

Amazon’s total costs were roughly $637 billion in 2025, implying that almost exactly one-third of the company’s cost base—between shipping and fulfillment—could be optimized by physical AI solutions that help speed last-mile delivery, reduce warehouse picking costs, and improve overall logistics coordination.

The company is already making some advances in this field with DeepFleet, but I’m expecting considerable year-on-year improvements as the business takes aim at its fixed costs.

To get a sense of the scale here, if you were to remove these expenses from Amazon’s income statement, the company would sport operating margins north of 40%:

Obviously, this isn’t feasible given that some of these costs are associated with things like payment processing, etc., but it goes to show you just how much is up for grabs within Amazon’s cost structure.

Base Case

Now let’s take a look at the base case that I see with Amazon over the next four years.

Functionally, it’s interesting to understand how much cost could be cut by Amazon, but realistically, I expect the firm will actually grow costs as it continues to scale revenue, and the impact of AI will not be on driving down costs but instead on reducing cost increases on a year-over-year basis.

It doesn’t take a lot here to really move the needle when it comes to both gross and operating margins.

In the model here, I’ve plotted out analyst expectations for total revenues for the next four years, which scale from $806 billion in 2026 to $1.1 trillion in 2029:

In this model, I’ve used a 7.5% annual growth rate in Amazon’s cost of revenues, reflective of the fact that almost 30% of the company’s costs here are associated with shipping, which I expect will become much more efficient. This, in turn, should translate to slower cost of revenue growth going forward.

This has a significant impact on gross margins, which - in the model - grow to 57% over the next 48 months.

Similarly, I model 5% annualized SG&A expense growth, again dampened by physical AI efficiency, given that SG&A is dominated by fulfillment, which makes up nearly 2/3 of the costs here, so I think it’s a reasonable assumption.

All of this drives operating margins higher to more than 20% by 2026, the expansion of which drives down the multiple, assuming a static stock price, to single-digit territory.

Finally, if you back out the numbers and assume a stable operating income multiple of 22.5, the base case implies 135% growth over the next four years and a market cap of roughly $5.2 trillion.

Again, I haven’t implied that AI will allow Amazon to cut costs while scaling revenues, but rather that AI efficiencies will drive very modest cost growth.

In this base case, Amazon presents as a Strong Buy, given the multiple avenues the company has for growth, management’s disciplined execution thus far, and the impact that AI might have on the company’s logistics networks.

Bull Case

In the bull case, I’ve knocked annual cost growth down by 2.5 percentage points both on cost of sales and SG&A.

Instead of 7.5% annual growth, I’ve modeled 5% growth for cost of sales, and instead of 5% growth in SG&A, I have modeled out 2.5% annualized growth:

In this case, Amazon gains physical AI efficiencies faster than the market or anyone currently predicts, which allows the company to scale revenues and volumes while methodically cutting headcount and replacing human pickers and packers with automated tools. It also implies significant expansion in the company’s shipping network, which finally begins to offer last-mile drone delivery, incredibly optimized route planning, and more.

As we can see, gross margins expand to over 60%, operating margins in 2029 are at more than 26%, and the multiple has been driven down to 7.6x. Backing this out with a stable multiple of 22.5, which is lower than where the market currently sits in the high 20s, we get nearly ~200% in capital appreciation over a 48-month span.

To me, these outcomes are not fully appreciated by the market, which makes Amazon's stock a Strong Buy at the current price.

Risks

I view the general risk to Amazon as quite low, as the company’s considerable scale advantages, incredible physical network, and general consumer lock-in make the company almost too big to fail within the retail landscape. Layer in AWS, currently the largest cloud service in the world, and it remains difficult to see how Amazon can be easily disrupted from its top spot.

That said, there are risks with my specific AI thesis—whether or not the efficiencies I’ve talked about here will actually show up in the numbers.

Historically, over the last four years, Amazon has averaged a cost of revenue growth in the single digits and SG&A growth mostly in the single digits, which tells me that the company is already enjoying considerable economies of scale:

However, if costs, for some reason, need to be structurally higher in order to support new global retail volumes, such that expense growth blows past my expectations, then it’s likely we won’t see the capital appreciation I’m anticipating over the next few years.

Summary

That said, I believe AI efficiencies within Amazon’s $200 billion-a-year-plus logistics network will make a serious dent in costs, driving up margins and structurally growing the company’s operating income, even if the multiple compresses somewhat in the coming quarters.

As a result, I’m upgrading Amazon from a Buy to a Strong Buy. I think that AMZN is simply a stock that everyone needs to have in their portfolio.

Cheers!

data.title: FCNCN: A 6.625% Fixed Rate -Reset Preferred IPO From First Citizens BancShares
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

This is yet another one of our series of articles targeting new fixed-income securities on the exchange. This time, our attention will be focused on First Citizens BancShares' (FCNCA) recent addition to the exchange - the 6.625% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Fixed Rate - Reset Preferred Stock, Series E (FCNCN). As usual, we'll start the analysis by going through the issuer's fundamentals to check out whether we want exposure to its preferred equity in the first place. After that, we'll continue with the sector's peer comparison to assess the investment qualities of FCNCN in a relative value analysis.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information regarding the new preferred stock. Here, we will focus on only the metrics that are most important for our analysis.

FCNCN pays a 6.625% annual non-cumulative dividend in $0.4141 quarterly distributions until its call date on March 15, 2031. After that, if the preferred is not redeemed, the annual dividend will be equal to the five-year U.S. Treasury rate on the applicable reset calculation date plus a fixed spread of 2.83%, resetting every five years thereafter. Dividends on the new preferred shares will accrue from Feb. 5, 2026, starting distributions on June 15, 2026. This means that the first dividend payment will be irregular in size and amount to approximately $0.5981. The preferred distributions are eligible for the preferential income tax rate. First Citizens BancShares' gross proceeds from the new security are $400 million, from a total of 16 million depositary shares issued with a liquidation value of $25 each. The new preferred stock issue received a credit score of Ba1 from Moody's and BB from S&P at the time of its initial public offering. FCNCN has no stated maturity date and may be subject to early redemption by First Citizens on or after March 15, 2031, on any dividend payment date.

As the issue was recently listed, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, FCNCN is priced at $25.01, with a current yield of 6.62% and a yield to call of 6.82% calculated by the XIRR function:

The forward yield curve shown above gives us a consensus expected value of 4.81% for the five-year Treasury yield in March 2031, when the first call date/dividend adjustment for FCNCN is. Based on these forward expectations, if the preferred stock is not redeemed, its annual dividend is expected to be adjusted to 7.64%, or 101.5 basis points higher than the fixed rate of 6.625% at the moment. Currently, the consensus expectation for the five-year Treasury yield in 2036 is to be higher than what it will be at the reset date in 2031. The forward yield curve is far from a perfect predicting tool. It's indicative rather than a certain thing, but this is what the market consensus is at the moment. According to this evaluation of the future expectations and the current pricing at par of FCNCN, we anticipate it to trade to its call date as a low-duration fixed-income issue. If some development changes the forward expected yield curve or a credit spread widening prices the security at a large discount, we'll need to reevaluate our expectations accordingly.

The Company

Founded in 1898 and headquartered in Raleigh, North Carolina, First Citizens BancShares is a financial holding and a parent company for First-Citizens Bank & Trust Company (First Citizens Bank). Through its divisions - General Bank segment, Commercial Bank segment, SVB Commercial segment (after the acquisition of Silicon Valley Bank), and Rail segment, First Citizens BancShares provides a wide variety of financial services to its clients, located mainly in North Carolina, Virginia, Georgia, Florida, California, and Texas. With the announced acquisition of selected branches of the Bank of Montreal in 2025, First Citizens BancShares will increase its footprint across the USA:

Company Performance

The daily chart below shows the market evaluation of the common stock of First Citizens BancShares, FCNCA, over the past 10 years:

We're going to compare the total return performance over the past 10 years of FCNCA to that of the general market, represented by the SPDR S&P 500® ETF (SPY) and SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE), as a representative for the regional banking sector.

The overperformance of FCNCA compared to the general market and the average regional bank cannot be doubted. Over the observed 10-year period, FCNCA has three times the total return of the S&P 500 and five times that of KRE.

The comparison to direct peers of similar size shows a similar chart. It's interesting to note that FCNCA has performed in line with the rest of the set, up until the bank crisis in March 2023. The acquisition of the SVB assets was, to a great extent, the event that triggered the price surge of FCNCA.

The question is, is the bank able to continue this exceptionally good performance?

Most of the SA and Wall Street analysts are expecting the price surge to continue, staying bullish on the company with a "Buy" rating. In contrast, the Quant Rating has recently revised its expectations on the future performance of FCNCA, downgrading it from "Hold" to "Sell", due to overpricing and negative EPS revisions.

Capital Structure

Below is the capital structure of First Citizens BancShares as of the fourth quarter of 2025. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also presented for comparison in the short term.

As of the end of 2025, First Citizens BancShares had total borrowings of $36 billion, senior to the preferred equity of $1.375 billion. FCNCN will increase the preferred equity by $400 million, to a total of $1.775 billion. At the lowest position in the capital structure is the common equity of $20.9 billion, covering the preferred shares close to 12 times.

The bank's CET1 ratio, as of the end of 2025, was 11.15%, a 50 basis points lower than at the end of Q3'25. The main reason for the decrease is the aggressive capital return strategy the management is employing:

Quite recently, the Federal Reserve confirmed that for First Citizens BancShares, the stress capital buffer remains 2.5 points above the mandatory minimum CET1 requirement of 4.5%. This means that despite the 50 basis points drop to 11.15%, the company is still comfortably above the 7% that the Fed requires of them.

Dividends

FCNCA has an impressive history of 36 years of uninterrupted dividend payments, the last nine of which have been increasing ones:

The twelve-month trailing dividend distributed to FCNCA's shareholders amounts to $8.1 in quarterly payments. With the last closing price of the stock at $1,988.55, we can calculate a 0.4% dividend yield. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past 10 years:

And the forward estimations for the dividend and dividend yield:

First Citizens BancShares has approximately 12.4 million common shares outstanding and a $8.4 forward estimated annual payment per share. We can calculate approximately 104.2 million forward annualized distributions for the common stockholders. The TTM distribution to the bank's preferred shareholders amounts to approximately $57 million. FCNCN's distribution will increase this number by $26.5 million to a total of $83.5 million. This means that the common shares' distribution is sufficient to cover the preferred shares' dividend on an annual basis, even after the increase in the preferred equity. The cushion is good enough for the preferred shareholders to feel reasonably assured that their dividends are well covered in the foreseeable future.

Credit Rating

ENGLEWOOD (S&P Global Ratings) March 4, 2025--S&P Global Ratings revised its outlooks on First Citizens BancShares Inc. and its primary bank subsidiary, First-Citizens Bank & Trust Co., to stable from negative. At the same time, we affirmed our 'BBB' long-term issuer credit rating on First Citizens BancShares Inc. (First Citizens) and 'BBB+' rating on First-Citizens Bank & Trust Co.

This is the beginning of the rating action with which S&P Global Ratings has affirmed the "BBB" long-term issuer credit rating on First Citizens BancShares and has improved its outlook from Negative to Stable. Below, we can see the summary of the credit scores S&P and Moody's have granted to the senior unsecured, the subordinated debt, and the preferred stock of the company:

First Citizens BancShares' debt

FCNCA has two fixed-rate debt issues trading OTC with maturity dates in '28 and '36, and credit spreads of approximately 85 basis points for the '28 issue and 125 b.p. for the '36 one:

Examining the OAS of US 'BBB'-rated corporate debt, as shown below, we can determine that FCNCA's debt is priced fairly to its peers with comparable credit quality. Creditors' trust is a good indicator for the company as a whole, and so it is for its preferred equity too.

First Citizens BancShares' Listed Preferreds

Besides the new issue, First Citizens BancShares has two other series of preferred shares outstanding - 5.375% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series A (FCNCP) and the 5.625% Non-Cumul Preferred Stock, Series C (FCNCO). Both of them are fixed-rate perpetuities, trading below par past their respective call dates, so not suitable candidates for a direct comparison with FCNCN.

However, we can have a look at the IPO risk premiums and see how the underwriters have evaluated the preferred equity of the bank in 2019-2020 and now.

FCNCP was issued in March 2020 when the COVID crisis had just unleashed its spread-widening effect on the markets, so I deem it natural that the security had a larger risk premium than its brothers. FCNCO, issued in November 2019, has an IPO credit spread of 3.2 points, or 40 basis points above the risk premium of FCNCN at the moment. It looks like the underwriters, as the S&P Global Ratings cited above, think that the bank is moving in the right direction and deserves financing at a lower cost than a couple of years back.

Sector comparison

The chart below compares the US bank-issued traditional perpetual preferred stocks priced above par, evaluated by their yield to call metric. I've selected the issues trading before their call dates, and with credit quality comparable to FCNCN's.

The metrics:

To me, FCNCN, with a YTC of 6.8% for five years from a preferred rated just under IG, looks fairly priced here, on a relative basis. We're usually searching for undervalued securities on a relative basis, rather than fairly priced ones, so we'll most probably skip on this one. However, for someone looking for exposure in the sector, FCNCN currently appears to be a better pick than the other recent bank-issued preferred stock, C-R.

Use of Proceeds

We expect to receive net proceeds from this offering of approximately $390 million, after the deduction of the underwriting discount and estimated expenses payable by us. We intend to use the net proceeds of the offering for general corporate purposes.

Source: FCNCN Prospectus

Redemption Considerations

The Series E Preferred Stock will be redeemable, in whole but not in part, within 90 days following a regulatory capital treatment event at a redemption price equal to the liquidation preference, plus the sum of (i) any authorized, declared, and unpaid dividends in any prior dividend period and (ii) any unpaid dividends for the dividend period in which such redemption occurs, whether or not declared, at the dividend rate on the basis of a full dividend period multiplied by a fraction, the numerator of which is the number of days in such dividend period prior to the redemption date, and the denominator of which is the total number of days in such dividend period.

Source: FCNCN Prospectus

Addition to the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF

The face value outstanding of the new preferred stock FCNCN of $400 million makes it a potential addition to the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index during some of its upcoming rebalancing. If that happens, it will also be included in the holdings of the main benchmark iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). PFF is an exchange-traded fund that seeks to track the investment results of the above-mentioned index and is rather important for us due to its influence on the behavior of many of the exchange-traded securities.

Conclusion

First Citizens BancShares has shown outstanding performance in recent years. Its capital structure is within the regulatory requirements, and its debt is priced in line with peers of comparable credit quality. In 2025, S&P improved the company's credit rating outlook. The dividend of the preferred shares has sufficient coverage. The new preferred share, FCNCN, is priced fairly relative to sector peers of comparable risk profile. I'd like to see it undervalued before opening a position.

data.title: Intuit's $100B Panic: Premature AI Death Call
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Intuit Inc. (INTU) has lost nearly $100 billion in market value over the past six months, as the “SaaSpocalypse” narrative has taken hold of the market.

Investors fear AI disruption across software, rendering a lot of companies potentially worth a lot less as their main offering, software, becomes commoditized.

But I believe this framing is fundamentally flawed.

Intuit is not being disrupted by AI, but rather, it is embedding AI into one of the deepest proprietary financial datasets in the world.

Recent results do not support the narrative that AI is displacing Intuit, and after the sell-off, this stock trades at a very reasonable forward valuation.

I’m taking this chance to initiate coverage on Intuit and buy the dip.

The $100 Billion Panic

It’s often said that price creates a narrative.

With Intuit falling over 30% in the last year, we have to analyze the narrative that has been created around this.

Why pay for TurboTax if an AI agent can handle your return?

We can break down this AI argument further into three distinct forms of disruption.

First, there is the argument that the “seat-based” SaaS model is obsolete.

In a world of agentic AI, perhaps you no longer need multiple accountants logging into QuickBooks. One manager supervising intelligent agents could replace several paid seats.

Second, Intuit’s partnership with AI model providers could compress margins.

There’s an argument to be made that, when Intuit integrates AI into its products, it is essentially just selling a ChatGPT model trained for finance.

And lastly, in a world of rapidly evolving AI, a small business owner could potentially soon create their own AI bot that can handle basic bookkeeping and tax preparation.

Each of these concerns sounds reasonable in isolation, but the flaw lies in what they overlook about Intuit’s business model and true moat.

Intelligence Vs. Financial DNA

The market is confusing intelligence with context.

Large language models are good at general intelligence.

Yes, they understand tax codes and financial terminology. But they lack persistent, structured knowledge of a specific user’s financial life.

Intuit, by contrast, operates on proprietary ”Financial DNA” accumulated over decades.

Across QuickBooks, TurboTax, and its other products, the company holds tens of thousands of data points per consumer and hundreds of thousands per small business.

This data is what sets the company apart.

When a generic AI categorizes a complex expense, it makes a guess based on patterns it has seen in aggregate training data.

But when Intuit’s system processes the same transaction, it incorporates years of prior filings, industry benchmarks, payroll behavior, and compliance history.

In an AI-driven world, raw intelligence is cheap and easily replaceable. But proprietary context becomes more valuable.

It's All About Outcomes

AI is disrupting, and it’s increasing competition, which can squeeze margins, but it is also creating a lot more value.

As I have discussed before, the new era of software will be about providing an outcome rather than a service.

Intuit is successfully shifting from selling tools to delivering outcomes.

Instead of simply providing bookkeeping software, it is moving toward “done-for-you” workflows powered by AI agents.

For small businesses, time saved translates directly into productivity and cash flow improvement.

Moreover, it opens up the door to new markets.

AI and Generative AI, in particular, has accelerated our opportunities to serve those customers and move upmarket.

Source: Barclays Technology Conference

We can already see this happening with the Intuit Enterprise Suite, which is growing around 40% and enabling Intuit to compete against larger players like SAP (SAP).

On top of that, as the platform becomes more automated and embedded, switching costs may actually rise.

Replacing a basic ledger is easier than replacing an intelligent system that handles payroll, compliance, invoicing, and forecasting autonomously.

The Human Element Matters

And yes, the human element matters.

Firstly, because AI is not ready to be deployed across companies just like that. Most people don’t have the time or knowledge to simply replace all their current enterprise software with custom-built AI.

The point is to make life easier for businesses, not create more work for them.

Ultimately, when it comes to financial reporting, avoiding AI hallucinations is pretty important, and so is having someone who can be accountable for their mistakes.

Earnings And Valuation

Of course, AI will change software. It may make some companies more valuable and others less.

But after a 30% haircut, I definitely think Intuit is worth a stab here.

This is a company still bringing in revenues at a very healthy rate, with a large market they can still tap into and healthy margins.

And the valuation now seems very reasonable.

The PE is 18, and the PEG is under 1.0, with the forward PEG just above 1.0.

This is a business with durable margins, strong free cash flow generation, and, in my opinion, deep data moats.

The market is anticipating something that has not happened yet, and I think at least exaggerated the AI doom narrative.

Final Thoughts

All in all, AI will reshape software, but I view it as a positive more than a negative.

There’s merit to some of the bear arguments, but there are also reasons to be bullish, and at today’s price, the risk-reward favors the upside when it comes to Intuit.

data.title: Bristol-Myers: I'm Buying Post Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (NYSE: BMY) has been one of the great winners in the pharmaceutical company since I rated it a strong buy back in late October, as it successfully executed its strategic rewiring designed to navigate one of the most significant loss of exclusivity cycles in the industry. I’ll dive into why I believe that, despite the stock gaining nearly 40% since I last reviewed it, the company is still prone to going higher.

Current Dynamics

BMY released its earnings earlier in February and came out strong with a double beat, as Q4 Non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.26, a beat of 6 cents. Revenue came out at $12.5B, a beat of $220MM, and a 1.6% Y/Y increase. More importantly, the growth portfolio has grown 17% Y/Y to reach $26.4B, now accounting for nearly 60% of total revenue. It has become clear that the trough of the company’s patent cliff is significantly shallower and more temporary than consensus estimates suggest. It is now large enough to absorb the generic erosion of assets like Revlimid and Sprycel, as it is currently demonstrating double-digit momentum.

Management has also demonstrated a strong ability to execute, as evidenced by the $1B in savings from the strategic productivity initiative in 2025, with another $1B targeted for realization through 2027. This discipline allows for debt reduction, as BMY completed a $10B paydown ahead of schedule. Plus, it has continued to find high-value business development, such as the acquisition of Orbital Therapeutics.

Diving deeper segment-wise, BMY continues to be a leader in oncology thanks to the durability of Opdivo and the emergence of next-gen combinations and modalities. Opdivo delivered $10B in annual sales for the year, supported by continued share growth in first-line non-small cell lung cancer. Furthermore, the success of Opdualag, which is a fixed-dose combination of nivolumab and relatlimab, is a critical component of the growth trajectory of BMY. It just achieved over $1B in annual sales and captures nearly 30% of the first-line metastatic melanoma market in the US.

On the Hematology side, the segment has drastically expanded through Reblozyl and Breyanzi, as well as the upcoming CELMoD agents. Reblozyl on its own generated $666MM in Q4, a 22% Y/Y increase thanks to its use in the first-line treatment of anemia in MDS patients. Breyanzi, which is a CAR-T cell therapy, also surpassed $1B in annual sales during the year, thanks to the FDA approval for follicular lymphoma and marginal zone lymphoma. I’m particularly enthusiastic about the CELMoD program, which will feature iberdomide and mezigdomide, where these oral protein degraders are designed to be more potent and better tolerated than existing IMiDs like Revlimid. The FDA has granted priority review for iberdomide with a PDUFA date of August 17. Success in this area could be a huge win for BMY, with the combination potentially reaching $3.6B by 2033.

On the cardiovascular front, the big crisis has been the looming patent cliff for Eliquis, which just generated $3.5B in Q4 for the year, though management's 2026 guidance for Eliquis growth of 10% to 15% demonstrates that the asset remains a potent cash cow in the near term. This actually surprised me, but this is further supported by the strategy to replace Eliquis revenue through Camzyos and the development of milvexian. In the case of milvexian, which is an oral factor Xla inhibitor being developed in partnership with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and intended to be the successor to Eliquis, it is set to achieve global sales of up to $6B by 2033.

Looking forward, the 2026 revenue guidance came in at between $46B and $47.5B, which notably exceeded the consensus of $44.2B. Though the legacy portfolio is projected to decline between 12% and 16% due to the removal of volume limits on generic Revlimid and the entry of generic Pomalyst.

Valuation

BMY’s valuation has always been part of my bullish thesis as the company generally was trading at the bottom of the industry metrics. Though the stock has increased by a vast margin over the past 4 months, BMY still has some of the lowest metrics in the industry, with a P/E Non-GAAP of 9.88, the second lowest in the group after Pfizer (PFE), and the same could be said for the company’s EV/EBITDA of 8.36. And though the company’s FCF yield is lower than in October, it still stands at an industry-leading 10.4%. The gap in valuation is still heavily linked to the patent cliff narrative, akin to Pfizer. Though compared to PFE, I believe that BMY is further in the cycle with an already strong performance in its growth portfolio that will outpace the losses linked to patent expiration. This lowered valuation also gives BMY a strong opportunity cost for patient shareholders with its current dividend yield of 4.13%.

The company successfully launched several therapies that achieved blockbuster status in 2025, with these brands currently growing at an incredible rate and being the primary engines offsetting the legacy generic erosion. For example, Camzyos grew nearly 60% in Q4 and is the first cardiac myosin inhibitor for oHCM and recently met its primary endpoint in a Phase 3 trial for adolescents, potentially expanding its market. The $14B acquisition of Karuna Therapeutics also positioned BMY as a leader in neuropsychiatry, with Cobenfy, the first muscarinic antipsychotic, being the centerpiece of this expansion. It surpassed 100,000 TRxs during the year, and the true growth potential lies in the expansion into Alzheimer’s disease psychosis, an area with 3.2MM diagnosed cases in G7 markets. Peak sales could eventually reach $8B annually in this case.

And though BMY is highly profitable like the bulk of its competitors, its gross margin is experiencing a temporary shift due to the transition from legacy to new growth. Gross margin is set to be slightly lower this year at around 69% to 70% due mainly to the loss of exclusivity for extremely high-margin products like Revlimid and Pomalyst. Though this is slightly offset by lower R&D and SG&A expenses, which dropped 8% and 1%, respectively. Though the company’s high Return on Equity is certainly attractive at +40%, thanks to the exceptionally efficient management.

Risks

There is still the patent cliff hovering over BMY’s head, with Revlimid already seeing sales plunge 55% in the final quarter of 2025. Eliquis, which is still growing, faces a US patent cliff in April 2028, plus the management is expecting a $1.5B to $2B revenue step-down in 2027. In all, LOEs could affect around 64% of BMY’s estimated 2025 revenue by the end of the decade.

On the regulatory side, the IRA has introduced government-mandated maximum fair prices for top-selling drugs, where already under pressure, drugs like Eliquis will see Medicare price negotiations begin in 2026. Plus, the agreement to provide Eliquis free to Medicaid in exchange for tariff relief materially alters the brand’s economics.

Conclusion

I believe that Bristol-Myers Squibb is a company that has navigated the eye of the storm, with the results of the year proving that the growth portfolio is not just a theoretical offset to legacy erosion but a tangible, high-performing reality that now dominates the company’s revenue mix. The ability to exceed the company’s conservative guidance while also reducing debt and increasing dividends demonstrates a level of operational excellence I’m willing to bet on. I keep my Strong Buy rating on Bristol-Myers Squibb.

data.title: Wyndham Hotels & Resorts Gets An Upgrade With A View, A Bright One
data.description:
One stock hitting my desk this week after its recent earnings results on Feb. 18th, when it beat estimates, is Wyndham Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (WH), which is also up around +6.8% since my bullish rating in January 2024.

What I loved about them then was stable dividend income and growth, the surge in travel demand forecasts in 2024, and revenue growth.

This time around, we'll be looking at more recent data and applying my 8-step methodology for a holistic follow-up on this stock. Because I often write about or invest in hotel REIT stocks, I think actual hotel brands like this and where they could be heading are also relevant to equity REITs in this sector, as well as a glimpse into the travel/lodging sector, which is a huge global economic sector.

Thesis Summary

My updated thesis this time actually reaffirms my prior Buy rating again and also agrees with the bullish consensus from both Wall St and SA analysts as of Thursday, despite the bearish view from the SA quant system.

Wyndham's key upside drivers could be a robust pipeline of room growth globally, a diversified brand portfolio across multiple spend categories, a strong EBITDA margin among peers that could drive earnings efficiency, as well as dividend safety/growth and positive upside forecasts to the share price. This bullish view comes despite the just-below investment grade rating from Fitch since last year.

The worksheet above summarizes my 8-step methodology, which reviews this stock over 8 categories to get a holistic score. Read on to learn more about each section.

Macro & Sector Outlook

First, let's consider some market impacts on the hotel sector.

Notable is a relatively confident outlook by JLL this month, who said the supply/demand dynamic could favor the hotel sector in 2026:

Slower supply growth across major markets will underpin performance of existing hotels, with most major U.S. cities showing construction pipelines below 2% of existing supply.

Further, while I've seen some comment that the 2026 World Cup could provide tailwind to hotels this year, which PwC also mentioned in an article, since that is a one-time tournament every 4 years I don't think it's the best sign of long-term sustainability but perhaps a near-term boost in the North America market.

The longer-term tailwind could actually come from AI use in this sector, which could have a direct impact on cost efficiency and thereby impact on earnings, not necessarily by eliminating the human element of the hospitality experience but by complementing it. For instance, PwC also wrote in the same study that:

On the operating cost front, AI is driving significant labor and operational efficiencies. Occupancy forecasting powered by AI allows hotels to better align staffing levels with anticipated demand, reducing overstaffing during slow periods and ensuring adequate coverage during peak periods.

Lastly, I would also consider the recessionary nature of this sector and the impact from consumer spending confidence, particularly higher-spending travel categories. This is relevant to Wyndham, which has a luxury and upscale component to their portfolio that includes the Registry Collection and Wyndham Grand.

My concern about that niche is that some survey data by Deloitte, published this month, showed that travel usage in the recent holiday season actually took a slight dip among the $100K+ income group, and this is actually a very busy traditional travel period of the year:

A mitigating factor for Wyndham, I think, is their highly diversified brand portfolio, which spans across different spending categories. Consider that Fidelity Investments wrote in their 2026 economic outlook that

"for all the doom-and-gloom about high prices that you might see in the headlines, American consumers aren’t letting it dampen their spending."

So, I am bullish in this section because despite the increased risk of lower upscale spend, the sector benefits from a supply/demand imbalance, could benefit from AI efficiencies, and has a resilient consumer.

Competitive Positioning

I picked the following 4 peers as good comparables for this stock: Marriott (MAR), Hilton (HLT), Choice Hotels (CHH), and Intercontinental Hotels Group (IHG).

The market cap range is wide, from $5B to $90B, with Marriott being the biggest and much bigger than Wyndham by market cap.

This niche has considerable focus on franchising their brands to others rather than actually owning all the hotel properties with their name on them, and Wyndham in their Feb. 2026 investor deck called this an "asset-light business model generating significant free cash flow."

Peer data does not put Wyndham in the best light, with a 5-year revenue CAGR at just under 6%, while Hilton and Marriott both exceeded 25%. Wyndham did somewhat better on return on equity (ROE) of around +58%.

In addition, I like to compare price return over the last decade vs a major benchmark like the S&P 500 (SP500) to get a sense of the potential momentum of a stock vs. peers, and what we can gather from the chart below is that Wyndham saw a positive price return of +32% in this period but severely underperformed the benchmark and all of the peers in this group, while Marriott and Hilton actually beat the benchmark:

Beyond these metrics, though, where I think Wyndham can compete in this niche is its continuing global growth beyond its home market of the U.S. For instance, its recent results press release said

"the Company's global system grew 4%, including 1% growth in the U.S. and 7% growth in the Company's higher RevPAR EMEA and Latin America regions."

Given the mixed picture above, I think it is worth being at least neutral in this section and not bearish.

Company Growth Factors

Next, let's talk about some more company-specific growth factors and impacts.

Even though it calls itself asset-light and its portfolio growth may not be tied to owning more property, like a hotel REIT may want to do, its growth can still benefit from new hotel developments that are planned to be future Wyndham-branded hotels, since it can drive future fees for the company.

In that regard, notable is the FY25 infographic showing a "record pipeline" across multiple categories:

Complementing this data is Seeking Alpha data showing expected forward revenue growth of +4.2%, beating the sector average and showing there is modest confidence about growth.

The Q4 results that came out this week also indicated "system-wide rooms grew 4% year-over-year," which is another key growth lever; however, new rooms and developments cannot ignore headwinds in another key hotel metric, RevPAR (revenue per available room), which saw a YoY decline. The company said, for example:

For the full-year, global RevPAR decreased 3% in constant currency compared to 2024, in line with the Company's outlook, reflecting a 4% decline in the U.S. and flat growth internationally. U.S. results reflected a 270 basis-point reduction in occupancy and a 120 basis-point decline in ADR.

So, the challenge for Wyndham for the rest of 2026 will be to show it can grow RevPAR again, and I believe a strong travel season this year could help. Notably, in their recent investor deck (pg. 18), the company has already forecasted improvement to RevPAR but also indicated some drivers of bookings this year:

So, Wyndham presents a growth case of potential recovery combined with pipeline growth, and for this reason I was bullish in this section.

Profitability Metrics

A key margin to compare with peers due to its impact on profitability and also giving us a sense of core business earnings efficiency is the EBITDA margin. If I had to choose one for this business, here is how Wyndham performed:

With an EBITDA margin of around +51%, Wyndham not only beat this peer group, but we also see the trend line has steadily risen since 2022, also indicating a nice recovery from the market shock to this sector due to the 2020/2021 pandemic era.

Unlike a hotel REIT, which might have property expenses as a key impact on earnings, this "asset-light" business of Wyndham does not even mention property expenses in its income statement, and we see from its balance sheet that property is not its biggest asset, but "goodwill" actually is.

Complementing this is consensus data showing estimates of between 6 and 15% YoY EPS growth this year and next, which I think shows confidence in this brand's ability to grow earnings.

We cannot, however, ignore the risk of one-time charges on this business when considering GAAP-based results, and it impacts headline metrics and shows in the recent results:

Full-year 2025 diluted EPS decreased 31% to $2.50 from $3.61 primarily reflecting non-cash impairment and other-related charges; however, adjusted diluted EPS increased 6% to $4.58, or approximately 6% on a comparable basis.

However, I am bullish in this section when putting aside those one-time accounting movements and focusing on the strong EBITDA margin, the adjusted EPS growth, positive analyst consensus, and the asset-light model.

Balance Sheet Risk

When it comes to balance sheet risk, being an asset-light business model does not mean without leverage, so here is a comparison of debt-to-equity vs. peers:

In this comparison, Wyndham's D/E of 4.50 is on the high end of this group, with only IHG considerably higher. However, this group also shows some companies with negative equity, like Hilton and Marriott, so there is a wide spectrum here, which means Wyndham is not exactly a major outlier.

Adding further to the risk lever is the fact that a major credit rating agency like Fitch has this company at just below investment grade at "BB+," which they reaffirmed in their Feb. update.

This comes after their May 2025 note, where they cautioned that "economic cycles and exogenous events can cause large declines in revenue and profitability for hotels."

So, I think a mitigating factor that could mute some of this risk is strong cash flow. Seeking Alpha data boosts confidence in that regard, showing expected fwd operating cash flow growth of nearly +13%, beating the sector average.

Further, from its investor deck (pg. 6), the company boasts the highest free cash flow yield among the peers shown, which is impressive:

Hence, I gave the stock a neutral view in this section because although they have a below-investment-grade rating and an elevated D/E above +4.0, they also have a strong cashflow story.

Dividend Outlook

Although not as high of a yield as some hotel REITs, Wyndham's roughly +2% dividend yield is almost in line with its sector average, so nothing remarkable.

Where it really impresses is not in yield but in growth, with a 5-year dividend CAGR of nearly +24%, easily beating the sector average, and also indicating to me a company with dividend resilience through the various cycles this sector goes through, considering that there exist peak travel seasons and slower periods.

In addition, with a dividend payout ratio of just 33%, this firm seems to have a lower risk of a dividend cut. Further supporting this is the positive EPS growth consensus for this year and next.

Hence, I am bullish in this section not for yield but for proven growth and dividend safety.

Valuation Metrics

Moving on, below is my worksheet using peer data from Seeking Alpha to compare valuation metrics but also to forecast a price target. Unlike a hotel REIT, whose important valuation metric is the P/FFO, in this case I'll be using the fwd P/E instead.

I am bullish in this section and calling this an undervaluation opportunity, and here is why.

I think the price/book comparison is less useful here due to a lack of adequate comparable data and this being an asset-light business, not one that holds a massive portfolio of owned hotels. So, the key metric is the fwd P/E, and Wyndham is undervalued compared to the peer average by 25%, even though I've shown earlier that it had the strongest EBITDA margin and also an analyst consensus pointing to potential EPS growth this year and next.

In addition, my price target implies a +7% upside, which is close to the +10% upside forecasted by the avg. Wall St rating consensus as of Thursday:

So, with undervaluation and both forecasts implying upside above 5%, I called it a Buy here.

Technical Charting

Moving past fundamental topics, let's see if the technical chart patterns will impact our thesis so far, which is overwhelmingly neutral-to-bullish.

In this chart, I circled a sell signal (death cross pattern) in red from last spring, and since then the 50-day and 200-day SMAs have been parallel; however, the 50-day seems to have pointed upward again as the share price spiked recently, likely in the wake of impressive earnings results.

In this case, I went with a Hold/neutral view because there is not a clear buy signal yet, just the early signs of strong momentum, but it could also retreat.

This neutral view lowers the overall score somewhat, but does not hurt the bullish case too much; it just makes it less aggressive.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

If I had to pick the one factor I think could have the biggest negative impact on my bullish thesis and directly challenge it, it would be an extended global recession impacting travel and lodging, which is essentially a discretionary spending category, and this can negatively impact room bookings and revenue.

A mitigating factor, I think, is that Wyndham has a global portfolio across multiple spend categories, and it is also likely that recessions do not have an equal impact globally but perhaps regionally or nationally. This is where I think the geographic and category diversification could help this company.

Conclusion

To wrap up, my updated review today reaffirmed why a bullish view should continue for this stock and what factors drive it, the most important ones being the robust room growth and development pipeline but also strong margins, in the context of being in a niche with some very big names like Hilton and Marriott, among others.

I view this stock as a dividend idea too this month, due to its proven dividend growth and safety, but it also remains an opportunity to own a piece of the hotel sector, while also understanding the unique risks of this sector.
